attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old woman is admitted to hospital for management of a large buttock abscess. She has end-stage kidney disease on maintenance haemodialysis. Her other medical conditions include type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Her medications include insulin, amlodipine, erythropoetin, sevelamer and calcitriol.

A Medical Emergency Team call is activated due to hypotension and fever. On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and temperature 38.9°C. There is a large, tender, fluctuant mass on her left buttock measuring 8 cm in diameter with surrounding erythema. Blood tests reveal a white cell count of 22 x109 /L (4-11) and C-reactive protein 280 mg/L (<5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most definitive step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of sepsis (hypotension, tachycardia, fever) in the context of a large buttock abscess. While several interventions will be required, achieving source control through urgent surgical drainage is the most definitive step in management. Deep tissue infections require adequate debridement and drainage for effective treatment, as antibiotics alone cannot penetrate well into abscess cavities.

Fluid resuscitation alone would be insufficient as it does not address the underlying cause of sepsis. While fluid resuscitation is important in managing sepsis-induced hypotension, it should occur alongside definitive management of the source.

Addition of vancomycin may be an important measure if MRSA is suspected, however, antibiotics alone would be inadequate for managing a large abscess. While antibiotics are an important component of treatment, they must be combined with surgical drainage to achieve source control. Antibiotics have poor penetration into abscess cavities.

Haemodialysis would not be the priority in this situation. While the patient may require dialysis as part of their ongoing care, addressing the source of sepsis takes precedence.

Hyperthermia management is not applicable in this case. Treating the source of the fever / high termperature with source control is the definitive step in management.

The management of this patient should include surgical drainage, empiric antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, and close monitoring. However, achieving source control through surgical drainage is the most definitive step in management.
learningPoints: Source control through surgical drainage is the most important definitive management in patients presenting with sepsis secondary to large abscesses.
Empiric antibiotics alone are insufficient for managing deep tissue collections.
references: Sigmon DF, Emmanuel B, Tuma F. Perianal Abscess. In: StatPearls [online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/books/NBK459167/

Beckingham IJ, Dannhorn N, Griffiths EA, et al. ANZHPBA position paper on the management of acute cholecystitis. ANZ J Surg. 2023;93(12):2916–2924. doi: 10.1111/ans.17750. Available at: https://doi.org/10.1111/ans.17750
